topic: EXP-088 integration: scorecard reframe, falsifiability section, std model clarification
our_argument_summary: Three surgical changes to close the review's biggest rhetorical vulnerability: (1) scorecard reframed from declaration to finding with cross-link to methodology; (2) explicit 'What Would Change Our Verdict' section with four concrete falsifiability conditions and an eclipse pre-commitment; (3) clarification of what 'Std Model Explains' means. Additionally, an evidence-roadmap div organizes the review into Tier 1/2/3 argument types.

holes_found.description: Derives_from_dome count mismatch in Condition 1. The falsifiability section (eg-falsifiability) claims 'only 4 of N claims even attempt a dome-geometry derivation (WIN-020, WIN-044, WIN-057, WIN-058).' However, wins.json code_analysis tags show derives_from_dome=true for only WIN-044 and WIN-057. WIN-020 (Lunar 18.6-yr cycle via gears) and WIN-058 (Unified angular coordinate) are both tagged derives_from_dome=false. This means the review's own data contradicts the falsifiability section's enumeration. A dome defender who checks our data files will find this inconsistency and use it to undermine the entire falsifiability framework.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) update the code_analysis tags for WIN-020 and WIN-058 to derives_from_dome=true if the analyst determined they do attempt dome-geometry derivation (in which case the tags are wrong), or (b) correct the falsifiability text to name only WIN-044 and WIN-057 and say 'only 2 of N claims.' The current state — where the prose says 4 but the data says 2 — is exactly the kind of sloppy bookkeeping we criticize the dome for.

holes_found.description: Eclipse pre-commitment discrimination window is extremely narrow. The dome predicts -17 to -21 nT (magnitude 17-21). Chapman predicts 5-20 nT magnitude. The discriminating sliver is only 20-21 nT — a 1 nT window. Any measurement of magnitude ≤20 nT falls within both ranges and is declared a win for standard physics by construction. A dome defender will argue this is a rigged test: we defined Chapman's range to nearly envelop the dome's range, leaving almost no outcome space where the dome can 'win.' The asymmetry is intellectually honest (the dome derived its values FROM Chapman data), but the narrowness of the discrimination window should be explicitly acknowledged.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief acknowledgment to the eclipse pre-commitment paragraph: something like 'The ranges overlap substantially because the dome's values were derived from Chapman-mechanism data. The discriminating window is narrow — approximately 1 nT — which reflects how little the dome's prediction differs from the standard model it claims to replace.' This turns the narrowness from a perceived weakness into a feature of our argument.

holes_found.description: The scorecard sublabel for '0 Survive Scrutiny' has become verbose. The original was one terse sentence; the replacement is now 3 sentences plus a hyperlink, running to ~80 words. While each addition serves a purpose (self-test framing, real-observations acknowledgment, methodology link), the combined effect risks burying the scorecard's visual punch under qualifications. The scorecard is designed for 3-second comprehension; the sublabel now requires 15+ seconds. Compare with the other sublabels: 'Claims Made' has 8 words, 'Acknowledged Failures' has ~20 words, but '0 Survive Scrutiny' now has ~80 words.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider tightening the sublabel. The key additions are the 'derives from dome geometry and differs from standard physics' criterion and the 'What would change this?' link. The self-test framing sentence ('This review does not assume globe geometry is correct...') could be moved to a tooltip, a footnote, or the evidence-roadmap, where it would be less disruptive to the scorecard's visual rhythm.

holes_found.description: Condition 3 (Schumann 7.83 Hz) states the dome geometry predicts 'approximately 22 Hz' but the SCIENTIFIC-CONTEXT.md and our self-contradiction analysis (Part 2) give a range of '22-35 Hz' depending on assumptions. The evidence roadmap div also says '22-35 Hz.' Using just '22 Hz' in the falsifiability section understates the discrepancy and gives the dome a smaller target to close. A dome defender might optimize for 22 Hz rather than confronting the full 22-35 Hz range.
holes_found.recommendation: Match the evidence roadmap phrasing: change 'approximately 22 Hz' to 'approximately 22-35 Hz' in Condition 3 to be consistent with the range stated elsewhere in the review.

stronger_arguments.description: Condition 1 could be strengthened by explicitly noting that the 4 (or 2) WINs that attempt dome derivation ALL fail the test — meaning the dome has already tried this path and the results disprove the model. This isn't just 'we haven't found one yet' — it's 'the attempts that exist produce wrong answers.' The current text says 'all four produce values that either match standard physics or contradict measurement' but could make this sharper: the dome's own best attempts at original derivation are its weakest claims.

The falsifiability section could note that the dome model's own 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' (referenced in the evidence roadmap) implicitly concedes our framework. If the dome ranks its evidence by strength, it acknowledges that some evidence is weaker than other evidence — which is exactly what our tiered evaluation does. This preempts the 'your taxonomy is biased' attack by showing the dome already accepts the principle of evidential hierarchy.
sources: evidence-roadmap div referencing dome's Steelman Evidence Ladder

kernel_of_truth.description: The curmudgeon reviews (HOLISTIC-HOL-NARRATIVE and HOLISTIC-HOL-STRESSTEST) that prompted these changes correctly identified a real vulnerability: a 0/N scorecard with no stated success conditions IS structurally suspect. The dome defender's taxonomy critique has genuine philosophical force — Karl Popper's falsifiability criterion applies to reviews as well as theories. By stating concrete conditions that would change verdicts, the review now meets the same standard it demands of the dome.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Making the review falsifiable does not make the dome's claims any stronger. The four conditions are straightforward and achievable in principle — derive 7.83 Hz from dome geometry, produce a genuinely prospective prediction, etc. The dome's failure to meet ANY of these conditions despite claiming 70 confirmed predictions is more damning, not less, when the bar is explicitly stated. The falsifiability section transforms the 0/N scorecard from 'we found nothing' into 'here's what we looked for, here's where we looked, here's what we found, and here's what would change our mind.'

advocate_mode.dome_defender_strongest_attack: A sophisticated dome defender would attack the eclipse pre-commitment as follows: 'You defined the Chapman range to be 5-20 nT, which nearly envelops our predicted range of 17-21 nT. The only outcome where we can win is if the anomaly exceeds 20 nT — a 1 nT window. Meanwhile, any result from 0-17 nT is claimed as a Chapman success even though it falls BELOW our predicted range. You've constructed a test where heads-you-win, tails-we-lose.' This attack has some force because it correctly identifies the asymmetry.
advocate_mode.our_best_counter: The asymmetry exists because the dome derived its -17 to -21 nT prediction by scaling Chapman-mechanism data from previous eclipses. The dome's prediction IS a Chapman prediction with a dome label. Any overlap between the ranges is not our construction — it is a consequence of the dome's methodology. A truly independent dome derivation from first principles would likely produce a different range, and we would evaluate that independently. We should be transparent about this rather than letting the defender discover the narrow discrimination window themselves.
advocate_mode.verdict: The attack is partially effective. We should acknowledge the narrow window explicitly.

cross_win_consistency.check: The evidence roadmap claims Tier 1 arguments (Parts 2, 2b) 'use only the dome's own stated parameters and equations.' This is consistent with Part 2's self-contradiction arguments (Schumann, tidal, gravity at rim, solar formula). Part 2b (code analysis) is more of a structural analysis than a self-contradiction — it examines the dome's code, not its equations. Labeling it Tier 1 alongside Part 2 is defensible but slightly misleading, since code analysis is about implementation quality rather than mathematical contradiction.
cross_win_consistency.issues_found: None critical. The Tier 1 labeling of Part 2b is a judgment call, not an error.

specific_patches.location: Line 1370, end of medicine analogy paragraph
specific_patches.fix:
Add

before the

A note on... paragraph
Reconcile the '4 WINs' claim with wins.json derives_from_dome tags. Either fix tags or fix text.
Change 'approximately 22 Hz' to 'approximately 22-35 Hz' to match evidence roadmap and Part 2 text

deeper_analysis:
The EXP-088 integration is the single most important rhetorical improvement to the review since launch. The falsifiability section transforms the review from 'we found nothing' to 'here's what we looked for, and here's what would change our mind' — which is the gold standard for scientific argument. The eclipse pre-commitment is particularly bold: it makes a public, timestamped, falsifiable claim about what we expect to happen. If the dome's eclipse predictions fall outside the Chapman range and match the dome's stated values, we've committed to upgrading. That's intellectually courageous. However, the execution has some rough edges. The derives_from_dome count mismatch (claiming 4 WINs attempt derivation when only 2 are tagged that way) is exactly the kind of data-prose inconsistency we criticize in the dome model. The narrow eclipse discrimination window needs explicit acknowledgment or it becomes a defender's talking point. And the scorecard sublabel is becoming a mini-essay when it should be a punch. The evidence roadmap is well-structured and the Tier 1/2/3 taxonomy is defensible. The key insight — that self-contradiction arguments are the strongest because they require no external frame of reference — is correct and well-articulated. A dome defender's only escape from Tier 1 is 'the math is wrong,' which is verifiable. The std model clarification (P4) is well-balanced. It acknowledges real observations, distinguishes attribution from correctness, and ties back to the medicine analogy. It does not overconcede — it says 'reproducing known results is necessary but not sufficient,' which is exactly the right epistemic framing. On balance: the scorecard reframe, falsifiability section, and std model clarification are major improvements. The minor issues identified (count mismatch, narrow eclipse window, verbose sublabel, missing

) should be fixed but don't undermine the overall argument.

citations_verified: Chapman mechanism reference (documented since 1933) — verified, S. Chapman 1931/1933 papers on magnetic effects of solar eclipses
Schumann 7.83 Hz — verified, standard reference value
Dome parameters H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619), disc_radius=20015, κ=1.67 — verified against inject_ai_layer.py and SCIENTIFIC-CONTEXT.md
citations_unchecked: WGS84 coordinate dependency of V12/V13 system (Condition 4) — plausible based on prior WIN-057/058 analysis but not independently verified this run
August 12 2026 eclipse date — not verified against astronomical records
